blocks.text: If z is a function of x and y, i.e., z = f(x, y), the total differential dz is obtained from the partial differentials dx and dy by the use of the following relation:

blocks.latex: dz = \frac{\partial z}{\partial x} dx + \frac{\partial z}{\partial y} dy
blocks.description: Total differential of a function z with respect to x and y.

blocks.text: The reason for this is more clearly seen if we work from the fundamental idea of function and change, and introduce the actually measurable quantities like z, x, and y.

blocks.caption: Figure 21
blocks.description: This figure illustrates the relationship between changes in position on the surface which is represented by the function z. It shows the differential elements in context.

blocks.text:
Thus, 

total change in z = change in z due to the change in x + change in z due to the change in y.

Let P be a point (x, y) on a surface, and let P move to a new position Q near to P. The change of position is most simply measured by the change in x and y; and this fact can be better illustrated by referring to a diagram (Fig. 21).
